items.question: Explain the difference between a p-value and a significance level α.
items.answer: α is a pre-chosen threshold for the Type I error rate (maximum probability of rejecting H₀ when it is true). The p-value is computed from data: the probability of seeing a test statistic at least as extreme as observed, assuming H₀ is true. Reject H₀ when p ≤ α.

items.question: What are Type I and Type II errors? How does statistical power relate to β?
items.answer: Type I error: reject H₀ when H₀ is true (false positive), controlled by α. Type II error: fail to reject H₀ when H₀ is false (false negative), probability β. Power = 1 − β is the probability of correctly rejecting a false H₀.

items.question: Walk through the intuition of a one-sample t-test. When would you use it?
items.answer: Use it to compare a sample mean to a hypothesized μ₀ when σ is unknown. t = (x̄ − μ₀)/(s/√n) measures how many standard errors x̄ is from μ₀. Reference the t-distribution with n−1 df for the p-value. Appropriate for roughly Normal data or moderate n via CLT, with independent observations.

items.question: Why can a result be statistically significant but not practically important?
items.answer: Large samples shrink standard error, so tiny deviations from H₀ yield small p-values. Significance does not measure effect magnitude or business value. Always report effect size and confidence intervals alongside p-values.

items.question: How do hypothesis tests relate to confidence intervals for a two-sided setup?
items.answer: For a parameter at significance α, a two-sided test rejects H₀: θ = θ₀ iff θ₀ lies outside the (1−α) confidence interval for θ. Tests give a yes/no at α; intervals quantify plausible values and effect size.
